clinicalStem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman at 40 weeks of gestation is in the second stage of labour. She has been pushing for 90 minutes with no advance of the presenting part. The fetal heart rate tracing shows a baseline of 170 bpm with late decelerations. On vaginal examination: cervix fully dilated, vertex presentation, OA position, station +2, membranes ruptured. The pelvis is assessed as adequate. She has an epidural in situ. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue pushing for a further 60 minutes and reassess
Proceed to emergency caesarean section immediately
Perform ventouse-assisted delivery in theatre with caesarean section standby
Apply Kielland's forceps and complete delivery on the labour ward
Administer tocolysis with terbutaline and observe fetal heart rate
explanation: The combination of prolonged second stage (90 minutes pushing in a nullipara), fetal tachycardia with late decelerations (fetal compromise), and favourable conditions (fully dilated, OA, station +2, adequate pelvis) makes operative vaginal delivery the appropriate intervention. Ventouse in theatre with caesarean section on standby is the safest approach because it allows immediate escalation if the instrument delivery fails. Continuing to push risks worsening fetal compromise, while proceeding directly to caesarean section without an attempt at instrumental delivery is not warranted when conditions are favourable.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing to push for a further 60 minutes is inappropriate given the pathological CTG with late decelerations indicating fetal compromise; delay risks fetal acidosis.
Immediate caesarean section without attempting instrumental delivery is not indicated when all prerequisites for operative vaginal delivery are met and station is +2.
Correct. Ventouse in theatre with caesarean standby is the safest management when fetal compromise complicates a prolonged second stage with favourable operative conditions.
Kielland's forceps are rotational forceps used for malpositions and require specialist training; they are not indicated for an OA presentation and carry higher maternal trauma risk.
Tocolysis does not address the underlying cause of late decelerations (uteroplacental insufficiency due to prolonged second stage) and would delay necessary delivery.
clinicalPearl: Operative vaginal delivery in theatre (trial of instrumental) with caesarean section standby is the gold standard when fetal compromise complicates a prolonged second stage and operative conditions are met.
keyClue: Station +2, OA, fully dilated, late decelerations — favourable conditions with fetal compromise
commonExamTrap: Students jump to immediate caesarean section, overlooking that favourable instrumental delivery conditions and station +2 make a trial of instrumental delivery the appropriate first step.
missedConcept: Prerequisites for operative vaginal delivery must be confirmed before proceeding; when met with fetal compromise, instrument delivery in theatre is preferred over immediate caesarean.
redFlagReason: Late decelerations with tachycardia indicate fetal compromise requiring urgent delivery; delay risks fetal hypoxia and acidosis.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman, para 1, is at 38 weeks of gestation. She is in the second stage of labour with an epidural. The fetal head is in the occipito-posterior position at station 0. The CTG shows a suspicious pattern with variable decelerations. The cervix is fully dilated. She has been pushing for 45 minutes. The registrar considers an instrumental delivery. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the appropriate management?
options: Ventouse can be applied immediately on the labour ward as station 0 is adequate for delivery
Manual rotation to OA followed by ventouse or forceps in theatre is appropriate
Kielland's rotational forceps should be applied on the labour ward by the registrar
Immediate caesarean section is mandatory for all occipito-posterior positions at station 0
Oxytocin augmentation should be commenced to aid rotation before any instrumental attempt
explanation: An occipito-posterior position at station 0 with a suspicious CTG warrants delivery but is not straightforward. Manual rotation to OA (either digitally or with forceps) followed by operative vaginal delivery in theatre with caesarean standby is the recommended approach. Station 0 is at the level of the ischial spines — the head is not sufficiently advanced for a safe non-rotational pull. Immediate caesarean is not mandatory if rotation can be safely achieved; however, the attempt should occur in theatre with full preparation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Station 0 means the head is at the level of the ischial spines; this is not an advanced enough station for safe non-rotational ventouse on the labour ward — theatre with caesarean standby is required.
Correct. Manual rotation to OA addresses the malposition, and subsequent ventouse or forceps in theatre with caesarean standby provides a safe pathway with a fallback option.
Kielland's forceps are a specialist technique, require significant expertise, and should not be applied by a registrar without senior supervision; they also carry a higher complication risk.
Caesarean section is not mandatory for OP at station 0 when rotation is feasible; a trial of rotational instrumental delivery in theatre is an acceptable first step.
In the second stage with full dilatation and a suspicious CTG, oxytocin augmentation is not the appropriate response; delivery should be expedited, not augmentation attempted.
clinicalPearl: Occipito-posterior position at station 0 in the second stage requires manual rotation to OA before instrumental delivery; any attempt should be made in theatre with caesarean section on standby.
keyClue: Occipito-posterior position at station 0 — malposition requiring rotation before traction
commonExamTrap: Students assume station 0 is adequate for any instrumental delivery, overlooking that malposition at the spines requires rotation first, not straightforward traction.
missedConcept: Station 0 is not advanced enough for a safe pull with ventouse or outlet forceps; malposition management requires rotation as a prerequisite.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman, para 0, is delivering at 40 weeks. The fetal head restitutes but does not rotate further, and the shoulders fail to deliver with normal downward traction. The midwife calls for help. The obstetrician notes the classic 'turtle sign.' McRoberts manoeuvre and suprapubic pressure have been applied for 60 seconds without success. The fetal heart rate was last recorded at 80 bpm before the head emerged. What is the most appropriate immediate next manoeuvre?
options: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby out
Attempt internal rotation using Rubin II or Woods screw manoeuvre
Perform immediate symphysiotomy
Replace the fetal head (Zavanelli manoeuvre) and proceed to caesarean section
Apply Kielland's forceps to the fetal head and pull with increased traction
explanation: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail to resolve shoulder dystocia, the next step is internal rotational manoeuvres — specifically Rubin II (pressure on the posterior aspect of the anterior shoulder to rotate it toward the fetal chest) or the Woods screw manoeuvre (pressure on the anterior aspect of the posterior shoulder). These aim to reduce the shoulder-to-shoulder diameter by rotating the shoulders into the oblique diameter. They are the recommended second-line manoeuvres in all major guidelines before more invasive options are considered.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia as it further impacts the anterior shoulder behind the pubic symphysis, worsening the dystocia and risking uterine rupture.
Correct. Internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II / Woods screw) are the recommended second-line manoeuvres after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed.
Symphysiotomy is a last-resort procedure performed only when all other manoeuvres have failed; it carries significant maternal morbidity and is not the next step.
The Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement followed by caesarean) is an extreme last resort after all other manoeuvres, including delivery of the posterior arm, have failed.
Increased traction on the fetal head risks Erb's palsy, cervical spine injury, and fetal death; this is explicitly contraindicated in shoulder dystocia management.
clinicalPearl: In shoulder dystocia, HELPERR: after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail, proceed to internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II / Woods screw) before attempting posterior arm delivery or last-resort procedures.
keyClue: McRoberts and suprapubic pressure failed for 60 seconds — second-line manoeuvre required
commonExamTrap: Applying fundal pressure is a very common and dangerous error in shoulder dystocia — it is absolutely contraindicated and worsens impaction.
missedConcept: The HELPERR algorithm sequence: after first-line manoeuvres fail, internal rotation precedes delivery of the posterior arm and precedes last-resort options.
redFlagReason: Shoulder dystocia with fetal bradycardia (FHR 80 bpm) is an obstetric emergency; delay or wrong manoeuvre risks fetal hypoxia, brachial plexus injury, and death.

sourceLecture: Breech presentation, transverse and oblique lie
clinicalStem: A 37-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 1, attends at 36+2 weeks of gestation. Abdominal palpation suggests a breech presentation, confirmed on ultrasound: frank breech, AFI 14 cm, placenta anterior and not low-lying, estimated fetal weight 2850 g, no fetal anomalies. Her previous delivery was a normal vaginal birth. Her blood group is A positive. Blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg and she is well. She asks about her options. What is the most appropriate management to offer?
options: Elective caesarean section at 38 weeks without offering ECV
Await spontaneous version until 40 weeks before deciding
Offer external cephalic version (ECV) at 37 weeks
Plan a vaginal breech birth given her previous vaginal delivery
Offer ECV immediately at 36+2 weeks under general anaesthesia
explanation: RCOG guidelines recommend offering ECV from 37 weeks in nulliparous women and from 36 weeks in multiparous women with uncomplicated breech presentation. This patient is multiparous at 36+2 weeks — she is approaching the recommended window. The appropriate step is to arrange and offer ECV at 37 weeks (or the next available appointment at/after 36 weeks for a multipara), as there are no contraindications: normal AFI, anterior non-praevia placenta, appropriate fetal size, Rh-positive. ECV reduces caesarean section rates and is the evidence-based intervention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Elective caesarean section without offering ECV is not the evidence-based first step; ECV should be offered first as it may achieve cephalic version and allow vaginal birth.
Waiting until 40 weeks for spontaneous version is inappropriate; spontaneous version becomes less likely after 36 weeks and delaying ECV reduces success rates.
Correct. ECV is recommended from 36 weeks in multiparous women; offering it at 37 weeks (or as soon as feasible at/after 36 weeks) is the appropriate evidence-based management.
While vaginal breech birth is an option in selected cases with experienced operators, it is not the first-line recommendation; ECV should be offered first to attempt cephalic version.
ECV under general anaesthesia is not indicated; it is performed without anaesthesia or with regional anaesthesia, and immediate ECV at 36+2 weeks without proper booking and consent is premature.
clinicalPearl: ECV should be offered to all women with uncomplicated breech presentation at or after 36 weeks (multipara) or 37 weeks (nullipara); it reduces caesarean section rates with a low complication rate.
keyClue: Uncomplicated frank breech at 36+2 weeks in a multiparous woman — ECV is indicated from 36 weeks
commonExamTrap: Students may immediately plan a caesarean section, forgetting that ECV must be offered first in uncomplicated breech presentation.
missedConcept: ECV timing differs by parity: multiparous women are offered ECV from 36 weeks, nulliparous from 37 weeks.

sourceLecture: Breech presentation, transverse and oblique lie
clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman, gravida 1 para 0, is at 38 weeks of gestation with a known footling breech presentation. She presents to the labour ward with a 'gush of fluid' from the vagina. On examination: pulse 98 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. Vaginal examination reveals the umbilical cord palpated below the presenting part, still pulsating. The fetal heart rate on cardiotocograph shows a baseline of 90 bpm with prolonged deceleration. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Administer tocolysis with terbutaline and arrange transfer to operating theatre
Place the patient in the knee-chest position and manually elevate the presenting part while calling for emergency caesarean section
Perform an immediate ventouse extraction on the labour ward
Insert a Foley catheter to fill the bladder and observe the CTG for 15 minutes
Administer oxygen by mask, site two large-bore cannulae, and await senior review
explanation: Cord prolapse with a pulsating cord and fetal bradycardia is an obstetric emergency. The immediate priority is to relieve cord compression by manually elevating the presenting part (or using the knee-chest / exaggerated Sims position) to take pressure off the cord, while simultaneously calling for emergency caesarean section. This manoeuvre must be maintained until the baby is delivered. Footling breech is particularly high risk for cord prolapse, making vigilance essential after membrane rupture.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Tocolysis alone without simultaneous manual elevation of the presenting part is insufficient; the cord remains compressed, and tocolysis addresses uterine contractions but not the mechanical compression.
Correct. Knee-chest position with manual elevation of the presenting part immediately relieves cord compression, and simultaneous preparation for emergency caesarean section ensures timely delivery.
Ventouse extraction requires full dilatation and a vertex presentation at an adequate station; this patient is nulliparous with a breech at unspecified station — ventouse is not applicable.

Bladder filling (retrograde bladder distension) to elevate the presenting part is a technique described for cord prolapse, but it is a secondary measure; immediate manual elevation and position change are faster and the priority.
Oxygen and IV access are important adjuncts but must not replace or delay the immediate mechanical relief of cord compression — this response does not address the cause of fetal bradycardia.
clinicalPearl: Cord prolapse management: immediately relieve cord compression (knee-chest position, manual elevation of presenting part) and call for emergency caesarean section — do not remove the hand until the baby is delivered.
keyClue: Pulsating cord below footling breech presenting part with fetal bradycardia — cord prolapse emergency
commonExamTrap: Students may choose tocolysis or bladder filling first, forgetting that immediate manual elevation of the presenting part is the first and most critical action.
missedConcept: Relief of mechanical cord compression takes precedence over all other measures; the delivering hand must remain elevating the presenting part until delivery.
redFlagReason: Cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia is a category-1 obstetric emergency; every minute of cord compression increases risk of fetal hypoxia, brain damage, and death.

sourceLecture: Breech presentation, transverse and oblique lie
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman, gravida 5 para 4, presents at 37+5 weeks of gestation. All previous deliveries were normal vaginal births. On examination, the uterine lie is transverse and the fetal head is palpable in the right iliac fossa. Ultrasound confirms a transverse lie with the placenta fundal, no evidence of placenta praevia, and adequate liquor. She is currently not in labour and membranes are intact. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Await spontaneous correction of lie and schedule a review at 40 weeks
Perform immediate ECV and, if successful, induce labour
Admit to hospital, attempt ECV, and if successful stabilise the lie; plan elective caesarean section at 38–39 weeks if lie remains transverse
Proceed directly to emergency caesarean section given high parity
Offer a trial of labour in a consultant-led unit with senior obstetric support
explanation: A persistent transverse lie at 37+5 weeks in a grand multipara is unstable and carries a high risk of cord prolapse when membranes rupture. Management involves admission to hospital (to prevent cord prolapse if membranes rupture unexpectedly), attempting ECV, and if successful, stabilising the lie. Elective caesarean section at 38–39 weeks is planned if the lie remains non-longitudinal. Awaiting spontaneous correction or sending the patient home risks cord prolapse at membrane rupture. A trial of labour with a transverse lie is not possible.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Sending the patient home to await spontaneous correction is dangerous in a grand multipara at term with transverse lie; ruptured membranes with cord prolapse is a life-threatening emergency.
ECV alone with immediate induction does not address the risk of recurrence of transverse lie in a grand multipara; admission and planned delivery at 38–39 weeks is required.
Correct. Admission prevents out-of-hospital cord prolapse, ECV is attempted, and if the lie remains transverse, elective caesarean at 38–39 weeks is the appropriate plan.
Emergency caesarean section is not indicated when the patient is not in labour and membranes are intact; elective planned delivery after ECV attempt is the appropriate approach.
A trial of labour is not possible with a transverse lie; vaginal delivery cannot occur unless the lie converts to longitudinal.
clinicalPearl: Persistent transverse lie at term in a grand multipara warrants hospital admission to prevent out-of-hospital cord prolapse when membranes rupture; elective caesarean is planned if ECV fails.
keyClue: Transverse lie at 37+5 weeks, grand multipara, intact membranes — unstable lie risk
commonExamTrap: Students may allow the patient to go home as she is not in labour, missing the risk of sudden cord prolapse if membranes rupture with an undiagnosed transverse lie.
missedConcept: Grand multiparity increases the risk of unstable/transverse lie at term; the principal danger is cord prolapse at membrane rupture, necessitating admission.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman at 36 weeks of gestation, para 0, is admitted in preterm labour. CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm lasting 5 minutes with no recovery despite repositioning, oxygen, and stopping oxytocin. Vaginal examination confirms the cervix is 5 cm dilated and the head is at station –2. The woman is conscious and haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate classification and target delivery time for caesarean section?
options: Category 2 caesarean section — deliver within 75 minutes
Category 3 caesarean section — deliver within 4 hours
Category 1 caesarean section — deliver within 30 minutes
Category 4 caesarean section — schedule as an elective at a convenient time
Category 2 caesarean section — deliver within 30 minutes
explanation: A prolonged fetal bradycardia (60 bpm for 5 minutes, unresponsive to intrauterine resuscitation) represents immediate fetal compromise threatening the life of the baby. This is a Category 1 caesarean section, which carries a target decision-to-delivery interval of 30 minutes. Category 1 is defined as immediate threat to the life of the woman or fetus. Category 2 covers maternal or fetal compromise that is not immediately life-threatening. The 5-minute unresolved severe deceleration fits Category 1.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Category 2 with 75 minutes is too slow for a prolonged unresolved severe bradycardia that represents immediate fetal compromise — this meets Category 1 criteria.
Category 3 (delivery within 4 hours) is for urgent but not immediately life-threatening situations; an unresolved 5-minute bradycardia to 60 bpm is immediately life-threatening.
Correct. Unresolved prolonged fetal bradycardia is an immediate threat to fetal life, meeting Category 1 criteria with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery target.
Category 4 is for elective caesarean sections at a planned time; this scenario is an acute emergency.

The 30-minute target is correct but Category 2 is incorrect; Category 2 applies to non-immediate compromise, whereas a 5-minute unresolved severe bradycardia is an immediate threat requiring Category 1.
clinicalPearl: Category 1 caesarean section (30-minute DDI) is indicated for immediate threat to the life of the mother or fetus — a prolonged fetal bradycardia unresponsive to intrauterine resuscitation qualifies.
keyClue: 5-minute unresolved bradycardia to 60 bpm after intrauterine resuscitation — immediate fetal compromise
commonExamTrap: Students confuse Category 1 (30 min) and Category 2 (75 min); selecting Category 2 delays care when there is an immediate threat to fetal life.
missedConcept: The four-category classification of caesarean section urgency: Category 1 = immediate life threat (30 min DDI); Category 2 = non-immediate compromise (75 min); Category 3 = urgent, no compromise (within hours); Category 4 = elective.
redFlagReason: Prolonged fetal bradycardia unresponsive to resuscitation is an immediate threat to fetal life requiring Category 1 caesarean section; delay risks fetal death or severe neurological injury.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman, para 2, undergoes a repeat lower-segment caesarean section (LSCS) for a second previous scar. There is dense adhesion of the bladder to the lower uterine segment. During mobilisation of the bladder flap, haematuria is noted in the urinary catheter bag. The operative field shows a 1.5 cm laceration in the posterior wall of the bladder dome. The baby has been delivered and the uterine incision is closed. What is the most appropriate immediate management of the bladder injury?
options: Leave the bladder defect open, insert a suprapubic catheter, and allow healing by secondary intention
Proceed to hysterectomy to obtain haemostasis and repair the urinary tract at the same time
Repair the bladder laceration in two layers with absorbable sutures, perform a methylene blue test, and leave an indwelling urethral catheter for 7–10 days
Consult a urologist and delay repair to a second laparotomy in 48 hours
Cauterise the edges of the defect with diathermy and close the abdomen
explanation: Intraoperative bladder injury recognised at the time of caesarean section should be repaired immediately in two layers with absorbable sutures (e.g., 2-0 Vicryl), ensuring watertight closure. A methylene blue or saline test confirms integrity of the repair before closing the abdomen. An indwelling urethral catheter is left in place for 7–10 days to allow healing under low-pressure conditions. Recognised and properly repaired intraoperative bladder injuries have excellent outcomes; the key is identifying them at surgery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Leaving the defect open to heal by secondary intention is inappropriate for a bladder injury; this will result in urinary fistula and peritonitis.
Hysterectomy is not indicated solely for a manageable bladder laceration; this would be over-treatment and expose the patient to unnecessary morbidity.
Correct. Two-layer repair with absorbable sutures, integrity testing with methylene blue, and 7–10 days urethral catheterisation is the standard management for intraoperative bladder injury.
Delaying repair of a recognised intraoperative bladder injury to a second laparotomy is inappropriate; immediate repair at the time of injury has superior outcomes and avoids a second procedure.
Diathermy cauterisation of bladder edges is not a repair technique; it causes further tissue damage and is contraindicated for closing full-thickness bladder injuries.
clinicalPearl: Intraoperative bladder injury during LSCS should be repaired immediately in two layers with absorbable sutures; methylene blue testing confirms watertight closure, and the catheter stays for 7–10 days.
keyClue: Haematuria and visualised 1.5 cm bladder laceration during repeat LSCS — recognised intraoperative injury requiring immediate repair
commonExamTrap: Choosing to refer to urology and delay repair is a common error; a recognised intraoperative bladder injury should be repaired immediately by the operating team.
missedConcept: Recognised intraoperative bladder injury must be repaired at the time of surgery; delayed repair risks fistula formation.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman, BMI 32 kg/m², has just undergone an elective lower-segment caesarean section at 39 weeks. She has no previous VTE history, no thrombophilia, and the surgery lasted 45 minutes without complications. She is now in the recovery area, is haemodynamically stable, and is able to mobilise. Which VTE prophylaxis regimen is most appropriate?
options: No pharmacological prophylaxis is required; early mobilisation alone is sufficient
Unfractionated heparin 5000 units subcutaneously twice daily for 5 days
Low-molecular-weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg) starting 4–6 hours post-operatively and continuing for 10 days, plus TED stockings
Warfarin initiated on the day of surgery with a target INR of 2–3
Aspirin 75 mg once daily for 6 weeks
explanation: RCOG guidelines recommend low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) for VTE prophylaxis after caesarean section in women with additional risk factors. This patient has an elevated BMI (32 kg/m²) and age ≥35, giving at least two risk factors which warrant 10 days of LMWH. LMWH is started 4–6 hours post-operatively (once haemostasis is confirmed), together with compression stockings (TED stockings) and early mobilisation. Warfarin is not used in the immediate postpartum period due to risk of bleeding and delayed onset.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Early mobilisation alone is insufficient in this patient who has BMI 32 and age 38 — these are additional VTE risk factors that mandate pharmacological prophylaxis per RCOG guidelines.
Unfractionated heparin is not the standard of care in postoperative obstetric patients; LMWH has a more predictable pharmacokinetic profile and is the preferred agent.
Correct. LMWH with TED stockings starting 4–6 hours post-caesarean is the evidence-based VTE prophylaxis for women with additional risk factors such as elevated BMI and age ≥35.
Warfarin is not appropriate in the immediate postpartum period — it has a delayed onset, requires INR monitoring, crosses into breast milk, and increases haemorrhage risk.
Aspirin alone is not adequate VTE prophylaxis after caesarean section in a patient with multiple risk factors; LMWH provides superior protection.
clinicalPearl: After caesarean section, LMWH should be started 4–6 hours post-operatively and continued for at least 10 days in women with two or more VTE risk factors; add TED stockings and encourage early mobilisation.
keyClue: BMI 32 + age 38 + caesarean section = multiple VTE risk factors requiring LMWH for 10 days
commonExamTrap: Students may choose early mobilisation alone, forgetting that additional risk factors (BMI ≥30, age ≥35) mandate pharmacological prophylaxis post-caesarean.
missedConcept: VTE risk stratification after caesarean section determines prophylaxis duration: 7 days minimum for all LSCS, extended to 10+ days with additional risk factors.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman, para 3, delivers vaginally at 39 weeks. She receives prophylactic oxytocin 10 IU IM at delivery of the anterior shoulder. The placenta delivers spontaneously and appears complete. At 30 minutes post-delivery, she has an estimated blood loss of 900 mL and continues to bleed. She is confused, pulse 118 bpm, BP 84/50 mmHg, RR 22/min, peripherally shut down. The uterine fundus is soft and palpable at the umbilicus. What is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE management sequence?
options: Request urgent blood transfusion, await cross-match results, and then administer oxytocin infusion

Perform bimanual uterine compression, administer further uterotonics (oxytocin infusion + ergometrine), and activate the massive haemorrhage protocol while securing IV access with two large-bore cannulae
Perform examination under anaesthesia to exclude genital tract trauma before starting uterotonic therapy
Administer 1 g tranexamic acid IV, insert a urinary catheter, and observe for 15 minutes to assess response
Insert a Bakri balloon and administer intramuscular carboprost
explanation: This patient has haemorrhagic shock (BP 84/50, confusion, tachycardia, peripheral shutdown) from primary postpartum haemorrhage with an atonic uterus (soft fundus at umbilicus) — the most common cause of PPH. The immediate priorities are: call for help and activate the massive haemorrhage protocol, bimanual uterine compression to stimulate contraction, two large-bore IV cannulae for fluid resuscitation and blood products, further uterotonics (oxytocin infusion + ergometrine as second-line), and urgent bloods including cross-match. All of these must occur simultaneously. Awaiting cross-match before acting, or delaying bimanual compression, is dangerous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Awaiting cross-match before giving any uterotonics or performing bimanual compression is a dangerous delay; resuscitation and uterotonic therapy must begin immediately in haemorrhagic shock.

Correct. Simultaneous bimanual uterine compression, further uterotonics, two large-bore cannulae, and activating the massive haemorrhage protocol addresses the cause (atony) and the haemorrhagic shock in parallel.
Examination under anaesthesia for genital tract trauma is a second-line investigation and is not the immediate priority when the uterus is clearly atonic; atony management takes precedence.

Tranexamic acid is indicated early in PPH (within 3 hours) and is beneficial, but it does not address the atonic uterus; it must be given alongside bimanual compression and uterotonics, not instead of them.

Bakri balloon is a surgical/uterine tamponade intervention used when medical management fails; it is not the first-line response, and intramuscular carboprost is a third-line uterotonic — both should follow rather than replace initial bimanual compression and oxytocin.
clinicalPearl: In PPH with haemorrhagic shock from uterine atony: activate massive haemorrhage protocol, bimanual uterine compression, IV access x2, uterotonics (oxytocin infusion + ergometrine), and blood products — all simultaneously.
keyClue: Soft uterine fundus at umbilicus + haemorrhagic shock = uterine atony as cause of PPH requiring immediate bimanual compression and uterotonics
commonExamTrap: Students may await cross-match or perform examination under anaesthesia first; in haemorrhagic shock from atony, resuscitation and uterine compression cannot wait.
missedConcept: The '4 Ts' of PPH: Tone (atony) is the most common cause; treatment sequence is bimanual compression → uterotonics → surgical options, alongside haemorrhagic shock management.
redFlagReason: Haemorrhagic shock from postpartum haemorrhage with uterine atony is immediately life-threatening; delayed or incorrect management leads to maternal death.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman, para 1, is at 40 weeks of gestation undergoing augmented labour with oxytocin for slow progress. She had a previous lower-segment caesarean section 3 years ago. She suddenly reports severe continuous abdominal pain that is different from her contractions. On examination: pulse 124 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg, abdomen is rigid and tender. The fetal presenting part, previously at station +1, is no longer palpable vaginally. The CTG shows fetal bradycardia at 60 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Stop oxytocin, administer IV fluids, and re-examine in 30 minutes
Perform amniotomy to expedite delivery and relieve intrauterine pressure
Stop oxytocin immediately, activate emergency response, obtain IV access and blood products, and proceed to immediate laparotomy
Administer a tocolytic agent and transfer to the ICU for haemodynamic monitoring
Perform ventouse extraction as the fetal head was previously at station +1
explanation: This clinical picture — sudden severe continuous abdominal pain, haemodynamic shock, disappearance of the fetal presenting part, and fetal bradycardia in a patient with a uterine scar on oxytocin — is uterine rupture until proven otherwise. Management is immediate laparotomy: stop oxytocin, activate the emergency team, secure two large-bore IV lines, commence fluid resuscitation, cross-match blood, and proceed immediately to theatre. Every minute of delay worsens maternal haemorrhage and fetal hypoxia. This is not a situation for watchful waiting or conservative measures.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Stopping oxytocin and waiting 30 minutes is catastrophic in suspected uterine rupture; the patient is in haemorrhagic shock with fetal bradycardia and requires immediate laparotomy.
Amniotomy is contraindicated; it will not relieve the situation and delays emergency laparotomy. The uterus has already ruptured — there is no role for amniotomy.
Correct. Suspected uterine rupture requires immediate laparotomy: stop oxytocin, emergency team, IV access, blood products, and urgent surgical exploration and repair.
Tocolysis is contraindicated in uterine rupture; it relaxes the uterus further, increases haemorrhage, and delays definitive surgical management.
The fetal presenting part is no longer palpable, meaning the fetus may have retracted into the abdomen — ventouse delivery is impossible and inappropriate in suspected uterine rupture.
clinicalPearl: The triad of uterine rupture: sudden severe continuous pain (change in pain character), haemodynamic instability, and loss of fetal station — requires immediate laparotomy, not reassessment.
keyClue: Previous LSCS + oxytocin + sudden continuous pain + loss of fetal station + haemorrhagic shock = uterine rupture
commonExamTrap: Students may stop oxytocin and observe, missing that haemodynamic instability and loss of fetal station mandate immediate surgical intervention.
missedConcept: Disappearance of the presenting part from the vagina is a pathognomonic sign of uterine rupture (fetus has retracted into the peritoneal cavity); this requires immediate laparotomy.
redFlagReason: Uterine rupture causes catastrophic maternal haemorrhage and fetal hypoxia; immediate laparotomy is required — any delay risks maternal and fetal death.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman, gravida 3 para 2, at 32 weeks of gestation presents to the emergency department with a sudden onset of painless, bright red vaginal bleeding. She reports no abdominal pain and no uterine contractions. She has had two previous lower-segment caesarean sections. On examination: pulse 108 bpm, BP 98/62 mmHg, uterus soft and non-tender, fetal heart rate 152 bpm. Examination of the vulva reveals active bleeding. Bedside ultrasound confirms complete placenta praevia. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilatation
Administer prostaglandins to ripen the cervix and plan delivery within 24 hours
Resuscitate with IV fluids and blood products, admit to hospital, administer corticosteroids, and plan emergency caesarean section if bleeding does not settle
Discharge with outpatient follow-up and pelvic rest instructions
Perform amniotomy to relieve pressure on the placenta and arrest the bleeding
explanation: This patient has a major antepartum haemorrhage (APH) from confirmed complete placenta praevia with haemodynamic compromise (tachycardia, hypotension) at 32 weeks. Immediate management includes: IV access and fluid resuscitation, cross-match blood and administer blood products, hospital admission, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity (32 weeks), and emergency caesarean section if haemorrhage does not settle with conservative management. Digital vaginal examination is absolutely contraindicated in placenta praevia as it can provoke catastrophic haemorrhage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Digital vaginal examination is absolutely contraindicated in confirmed placenta praevia; it can disrupt the placental edge and cause catastrophic haemorrhage. Ultrasound assessment is used instead.
Prostaglandins are used for cervical ripening and labour induction — they are absolutely contraindicated in placenta praevia as they cause uterine contractions that can worsen haemorrhage.

Correct. Resuscitation, admission, corticosteroids for prematurity at 32 weeks, and emergency caesarean section if bleeding does not settle is the appropriate management for APH from placenta praevia with haemodynamic compromise.
Discharging a patient with haemodynamic compromise from active placenta praevia bleeding is dangerous and inappropriate; this patient requires admission and active management.
Amniotomy has no role in the management of placenta praevia haemorrhage and will exacerbate bleeding by removing the protective pressure of the fetal membranes.
clinicalPearl: In placenta praevia with APH: NEVER perform a digital vaginal examination; resuscitate, admit, give corticosteroids if preterm, and plan caesarean section — conservative management only if bleeding settles and patient is stable.
keyClue: Painless bright red bleeding + soft non-tender uterus + confirmed complete placenta praevia + haemodynamic compromise
commonExamTrap: Digital vaginal examination in placenta praevia is the classic fatal error trap in exam scenarios — it is absolutely contraindicated.
missedConcept: Placenta praevia APH is always managed by caesarean section; digital examination is lethal, and the contraindication to vaginal delivery is absolute in major praevia.
redFlagReason: Major APH from placenta praevia with haemodynamic instability can lead to maternal death; digital VE is a potentially fatal error that can trigger catastrophic haemorrhage.
